4 – 2x4 or 2x6 @ 7” (can be cut longer for longer legs, use a 2x6 for a more modern look) - bed legs
21 – 1x3 @ 27"  slats are cut in half to maximize materials of an 8' long board or 10 - 1x3 @ 54" - bed slats
4 – 2x4 or 2x6 @ 7” (can be cut longer for longer legs, use a 2x6 for a more modern look) - bed legs
21 – 1x3 @ 30"  slats are cut in half to maximize materials of an 8' long board or 10 - 1x3 @ 60" - bed slats
4 – 2x4 or 2x6 @ 7” (can be cut longer for longer legs, use a 2x6 for a more modern look) - bed legs
4 – 2x4 or 2x6 @ 7” (can be cut longer for longer legs, use a 2x6 for a more modern look) - bed legs

steps.body:
Build the 2x6 frame using two screws per corner, 2-1/2" long to 3" long self tapping star bit screws (spax or similar).
Adjust the frame for square by taking opposite diagonals and making sure the two diagonals match.
Attach cleats to inside for supporting the slats in later steps. Use 2-1/2" screws and glue, screws about every 8-12".
This middle support is important for full size or larger beds. Attach so top is level with top of cleats, as bed slats will sit on top.  Use two screws per end.

Now we can start trim out. Add trim so it's flush on insides and at head of bed. Attach with either 1-1/2" long self tapping screws or brad nails and glue.
TIP: If you have a Kreg Jig, building the frame first with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, will result in a nicer overall finished bed.

Measure and cut the trim for the top and bottom, with the 1x2 trim at the top.
Attach with nails or the shorter screws and glue.

For the bed legs, cut the legs from either 2x4 or 2x6.  The 2x6 will result in a stronger bed leg.
You can taper the legs, or even use store bought metal bed feet.
Attach to base of bed, use 3 screws per leg, with glue.
For full size and larger, flip the bed over and measure and cut center legs to support the center 2x4 and attach. (see step 8)

Now add the slats. Slat placement and number of slats is dependent of your mattress requirements. Screw the slats down with the shorter screws.
To help take any wobble out of the bed, on a end slats, screw down with four screws (two per end)
If you find your bed needs a little extra support in the center, here's the best way to beef it up.

title: 2x4 Easy Build Garage Shelves [Freestanding]
shopping_list: 2 - 10' long 2x4s PER Leg Support (I had 5 leg supports, so I bought 10)
4 - 2x4 - length of shelf PER SHELF (I bought 16 2x4 @ 20' long because I had 4 shelves, 20' long each)
2-1/2" self tapping wood screws (star bit don't strip), two per leg/per shelf board
Additional screws for anchoring to wall to prevent forward tipping or racking

steps.body: Mark out placement of shelf supports on each of the legs.  Attach legs to shelf supports with 2-1/2" self tapping screws and glue.

Mark out on 4 of the shelf boards the placement of the leg supports.  Attach 2 to top shelf and 2 to bottom shelf with screws.  I attached the outer boards first, flush to the inside of the leg boards.  This will create the basic shape of your shelving unit.
Adjust for square.  Then add remaining shelf boards and screw down.
Attach securely to wall to prevent forward tipping or racking.

title: Rustic Wedding Ceremony Arch Plans
shopping_list: 2-1/2" self tapping wood screws (about 20, SPAX or similar brand)
1-3/4" self tapping wood screws (about 10, SPAX or similar brand)
cut_list: 2 - 2x4 @ 20-1/4" - long point to long point measurement, both ends cut at 45 degrees off square, ends not parallel
2 - 2x4 @ 24-1/2" - long point to long point measurement, both ends cut at 45 degrees off square, ends not parallel

steps.body: Attach the legs to the base wit 2-1/2" screws.
Attach the cross bracing to the base of the legs as shown.
Mark the overhangs on the 1x4 and attach to the bases with the 1-3/4" screws.
Attach the cross bracing as shown, using the 1-3/4" screws at the top.  A mending plate or pocket holes can be uses to fasten at the base.

title: Small Modern Chicken Coop
shopping_list: 15 - 5-1/2" wide x 72" long cedar fence pickets
20 feet of 36" wide chicken wire or hardware cloth
15 feet of 24" wide chicken wire or hardware cloth
120 - 1-1/4" to 1-3/4" length - self tapping star bit screws
staples for attaching chicken wire or hardware cloth
cut_list: 2 - 2x4 @ 94-1/2" - both ends cut at 5 degrees off square, ends are parallel, long point to short point measurement
2 - 2x4 @ 61" - one end cut at 5 degrees off square, long point measurement*
2 - 2x4 @ 58-1/4" - one end cut at 5 degrees off square, long point measurement*
2 - 2x4 @ 53-1/4" - one end cut at 5 degrees off square, long point measurement*
2 - 2x4 @ 56" (shown as 56-3/8", but reduced to allow door swing clearances)*

steps.body:
Layout the two side frame pieces, so they are in mirror.
Mark out all pocket holes.
Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes on ends as shown in diagram.
Attach with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws.
Build the second frame in mirror.
TIP: It was a super paint o paint after adding the chicken wire.  I would recommend a quick coat of finish on the insides of the frame before adding chicken wire.
Attach chicken wire or hardware cloth to insides with staples.  Trim back if necessary and fold edges over.
Add the 2x2 floor cleat to the insides of the side frame with the longer screws.

In the video I used 1/2" thick exterior plywood instead of cedar fence pickets.  This can be done, but there is alot of waste and alot of cutting.  If you opt for the plywood instead of the cedar fence pickets, you will need two full sheets.
Cut the first cedar fence picket with the top at 5 degrees, long point measurement, as shown.  Attach to inside of the side walls, flush to the top of the 2x2.  There should be minimum 1-1/2" space above the cedar fence picket.

Measure the short point length of the first cedar fence picket and use this measurement to cut the next cedar fence picket.  You may need to use a new cedar fence picket for the next cut, but eventually will be able to start using the scrap ends as the cuts get shorter.
Attach with the shorter star bit screws, two screws per joint.

Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes on each end of the 33" 2x4s.  Attach as shown in the diagram.
NOTE: Another 2x4 can be added to the bottom end, I left this off to make cleaning easier but it is shown in the photos.
Flip over on to the second side panel and attach with the pocket hole screws.
Add the 2x2s to the inside to trim out the interior wall.

Add the cedar fence pickets to the insides with the short star bit screws.  Save the scrap pieces for the door.
This opening acts as both a vent and door.  Because it leads into an enclosed run, I did not add a door for cover.

Cut the floor and set in place.  I did measure and cut the floor to fit, the floor was cut about 1/2" smaller so it would be easy to remove.  I did not screw it down, so it can be removed if needed.
NOTE: In the video the floor was able to slide out.  I improved the plans by adding the lip on the back to keep bedding from falling out when the door is opened.
Build the door frame with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws.  Attach chicken wire to the back.  Install to the coop with hinges, use a clasp to lock closed.

Build the back door, cover the upper portion in cedar fence pickets and the lower portion in chicken wire. Install with hinges and a latch.
NOTE: I did improve the plan with the full door.  This helps with cleaning the lower level and is a better built door system.
This chicken coop is designed to work with an off the shelf sheet of tin, 8 feet long.  Simply slide on top and screw down to the 2x4s in the roof.
The nesting box can be built using scrap 3/4" plywood.  You can assemble with pocket holes or with the 2-1/2" screws.

title: Wood Seesaw or Teeter Totter
shopping_list: 1 – 10” 5/8” bolt with nut and four washers
cut_list: 2 – 2x6 @ 24” (short base - both ends cut at 30 degrees off square ends NOT parallel to each other)
2 – 2x6 @ 12” (scrap piece can be shorter than 12” – used for handle)
FROM EACH 2x6 cut one each of the 24”, 36”, 24” and 12” to conserve boards

steps.body: Attach long base supports to uprights with lots of screws and glue.
Then add the side supports again with lots of screws and glue.
Build the seat part by sandwiching the handle supports between 8 foot long 2x4s and attaching with glue and lots of screws. Drill a 5/8" hole in center of 2x4s for the pivot bolt.

I used scrap 1x8 boards for the seats, covered in a foam chair pad cut in half and outdoor fabric. I drilled three holes in the uprights with 5/8" bit so the seesaw could be adjustable. The most difficult part of this seesaw was getting the bolt holes lined up. We ended up just making the bolt holes a little larger so the bolts could pass through easier. On the bolt, we put washers on each side of the uprights to protect the finish and for a smoother ride. For the handles, we had extra closet rod dowel, so we just cut to size, and drilled a hole and inserted the rod. We then glued and screwed in place.

title: Modern Single Outdoor Chaise Lounge
description: Here's Some Tips to Build Better Outdoor Furniture
shopping_list: 2 sets of 3" hinges suitable for exterior use

steps.body:
To build this lounger, you can either use the
Kreg Jig
(pocket hole recommendations shown above) or self tapping wood screws.
Pocket Holes
The advantage of pocket holes is no visible fasteners.  If you are using a cushion, this will cover all the fasteners, so this may or may not be as important to you.
When joining 2x to 2x boards, the setting is 1-1/2" with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws.  When joining 2x to 1x boards, use the 3/4" setting and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.
Mark out and drill all pocket holes prior to assembly.  The assembly order may differ than shown in diagrams
Build the frame, using either 2-1/2" screws or the Kreg Jig. Make sure that the frame is very square.
Attach legs to the outside of the frame with 2-1/2" screws.
Screw cleats inside the frame, four screws per cleat.  2-1/2" self tapping screws.
Attach decking to the top of the chaise lounge with 2" self tapping screws.
Build the back piece with 2" screws into the backs of the 2x2s.

Build the back support as shown.  Make sure the 2x4s are set in 2" from the ends of the 2x2s.
Hinge to the back rest.
Hinge the entire back piece inside the chaise lounge opening.

Install the stop blocks as shown.
You can alter the locations, just make sure the stop blocks don't interfere with the back closing flat.
Now take some of the 1x2 scraps and create stops as shown above to hold the prop up in place. You can space these at any desired location.

title: Simple Playhouse Deck - Free Plans
description: Simple Playhouse Plans with Deck COMPLETE PDF BUNDLE PDF bundle for all walls and deck • master shopping list • cut list
shopping_list: 2 – 4×4 Posts 8′ Long (3 if you are using the swingset option)
29 – 2×6 Boards, 8′ Long (8 of these can be stud length 92 5/8″ if stud length is cheaper)
cut_list: 4 – 4×4 Posts @ 47″ (OR 2 @ 47″ and 2 @ 96″ for the swingset option)
8 – 2×6 @ 12″ (Diagonal Supports, both ends mitered down 45 degrees)

steps.body: Sides { Part 1 } Lay the 4×4 posts flat and attach the posts as shown above to the sides. Use 3″ screws. Also note that if you are building the swing arm, ONE of each of the posts on each side will be 96″ long.

Sides { Part 2 } Now attach the remaining sides to the posts and sides as shown above. Use 3″ screws. Level the frame and adjust for square. Trust me, it will be a miracle if it’s square without adjustment.
Diagonal Supports With the box nice and square and level, fasten the diagonal supports to each corner as shown above. This should stiffen up the corners considerably.
Joists Mark out all of your joists on the sides, (the long sides) as shown above. Then screw the joists to the sides as shown above. Use 3″ screws.
Cross brace the base with 2x4s as shown in diagram, attaching with at screws into the framing.

title: Cedar Fence Picket Raised Garden Beds - Narrow Width
shopping_list: 1x2 or 1x3 Cedar boards for corners (if you don't have a tablesaw)
1-1/4" self tapping exterior star bit screws
Garden stakes or concrete stakes (we used a couple of stakes on each bed just to keep the bottoms in place)
cut_list: 4 – 1×6 Fence Pickets @ 72″ (Side Panels, you can trim the dog ear off and work with a 71″ Fence post)
Optional Top Trim - CUT TO FIT
2 – 1×2 Fence Pickets @ 72″ (Top Trim, I used the non-dogeared ones from the center of the cuts)

steps.body:
Optional: Rip your Corner Posts
I’ve done the math, and by ripping one fence post into 4 – 1 1/4″ wide strips, you are saving quite a bit of money (well, that is, if you intend to build a garden full of planters). So set your tablesaw to 1 1/4″ and rip one of the fence posts to 1 1/4″ wide, as shown above.
Or Use 1x2 Cedar Boards
If you don’t have a table saw, you can use 1×2 cedar boards.

Side Panels
Use your 1″ screws and glue to put together your side panels as shown above. The post will overextend the sides by 5/8″ as shown above.
I also used my
Kreg Jig™
to join the boards together in the center (optional) or you can use a 1x2 in the center.

End Panels
Build your end panels exactly like your side panels.

Assembling the Panels
The panels should fit together like a puzzle. Fasten with 2″ screws and glue. Check for square.
OPTIONAL: Measure and cut your top trim to fit.  Attach to the top.

title: Essential Entryway Bench
shopping_list: 1x3s for bay supports and trim, you'll need approximately 5 times the overall width of the bench
Primer and paint or stain for finishing
cut_list: Carcass cuts are determined through the configuration Ana's Design App in Step 1.
Bay supports are equal to the width of your bays.
Top and bottom trim should be cut to fit for a perfect trimout.

steps.body:
Design Your Bench
Use our
free configurator tool here
to design your bench carcass, here are the parameters to set:
Depth
- recommend 15-1/4" depth, but can use 20-1/4" and 23-1/4" too
Width
- any width up to 90"
Height
- recommend 18"
Bays
- as many bays as desired, but keep bay width less than 36" as it is supportive of the top and will keep the top from sagging
Top
- yes
Bottom
- yes
Space Under Bottom
- 2-1/4" for 1x3 base trim (as I did) or 3-1/4" for 1x4 base trim
Click here to use this tool in a pop-up full page view

Creating a Cut List and Shopping List from Your Configuration
Once you have your configuration done and the drawing printed, use the drawing to create a cut list by printing it out, and drawing all the added parts on.
Once you have a cut list, you can create a shopping list from the cut list.
Cutting the Plywood Carcass Pieces
Rip the 3/4" plywood into strips the desired depth of the bench.  Then cross cut the strips to create the pieces needed in the plans.
Apply edge banding to the front edges of the pieces and trim off with a razor.  Sand rough edges.

Main Carcass Assembly
On the bottom shelf, drill three 3/4" pocket holes at each end (four if you went with a 23-1/4" wide carcass).
Attach bottom shelf to the two sides, with 2-1/4" gap underneath, using 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.
Then attach dividers to bottom shelf using 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue.  TIP: Use the bay supports as spacers to guide you in placing the dividers as shown in the video.

Top Bay Supports
You'll need some support at the top.  Cut 1x3s to match the bay widths and attach as shown with pocket holes.
TIP: Also drill pocket holes facing upward on the back bay supports for attaching the top in later steps.

Bottom Support
For longer benches, you may need additional support in the middle to keep the bench from sagging.  You can either add a center "leg" as I did in the video, or add some blocking as shown here - the base trim will attach to it and work as the support.

Base Trim - Sides
Measure and cut 1x3 base trim for the sides and nail and glue on with 1-1/4" nails.

Base Trim - Front
Measure and cut the front base trim and nail and glue on, make sure you nail to the hidden support blocking (if used) and the side trim.

Top Back Trim
Measure and cut the back 1x3 trim and nail and glue to the back.

Top Side Trim
Measure and cut the side top trim.  Cut the top front corner at an angle (optional).
Nail and glue on with 1-1/4" brad nails.

Attaching Top
TIP: It may be easier to attach the top after painting or staining.
The top is attached with equal overhangs on the sides and flush on the back.
Attach with 1-1/4" screws (yes you can use the pocket hole screws) through the bay supports.

title: U Shaped Table or Desk for School
shopping_list: 2" self tapping star bit wood screws (about 50)
primer and paint or wood stain
polyurethane for desk top is recommended for durability

steps.body: Build two frames as shown. For greater strength, predrill and use 2" screws.
Attach the back apron board as shown to the two pieces built in step 1.
Attach the inner support frame piece as shown.
Build six legs by attaching 2x2s to 2x4s.
Attach legs to the corners of the desk frame with multiple 2" screws and glue, using a level to make sure the legs are attached level.
Add blocking as shown.

Position the 1x10 desktop pieces on top and screw down.
TIP: For a nicer top, build the entire top first with pocket holes, then attach the completed top to the frame.
Add the remaining desktop pieces and screw down.

title: Modular Workbench Sawhorses Countertops
shopping_list: 5 - 2x4 @ 10 feet long (preferably yellow toned (not red toned))
70 - 2-3/4" self tapping flat head wood screws
20 - 1-1/4" self tapping flat head wood screws

steps.body: Measure and mark the legs 12" up from the bottom.  Use a speed square to transfer mark across.  Clamp and attach legs to the 45" 2x4 boards, using 2-3/4" screws. Build two legs sets that are identical.
Attach the 2x4s at 21" to the legs sets, to create the top frame, with two screws per joint.
Repeat for the bottom frame.

Find the center of the bottom shelf.
Find the center of a 21" 2x4 board.
Match centers and attach with 2-3/4" screws, two per joint.
Center two more slats in the bottom shelf, on the openings.  Attach with 2-3/4" screws.
Center the remaining slats in the openings and attach with 2-3/4" screws.
Place the plywood panel on top.  Adjust the base so the plywood top matches the base on side edges.  Screw down with 1-1/4" screws, every 12".

One of the biggest reasons to build your own projects is the ability to customize for your specific needs.
Supplies: 2" swivel caster wheels, 1-1/4" screws
Measure the caster wheels to determine how much to cut off the legs of the workbench.  Mark and cut the legs down on the workbench.  Install the caster wheels.

title: Free 8-Foot Garage Shelving Plan – Sturdy, Affordable, and Easy to Build
shopping_list: 2 - 12 foot long 2x4s, cut in half exactly
3 - 8 foot or stud length 2x4s, use these boards to cut the 21" long pieces
2 - 1/2" sheets of plywood, cut in half lengthwise
Extra support - 2-6 1x4 boards, 72" long and additional 1-3/4" screws
TIP: For lighter weight, easier to build shelving, use the light toned pine or spruce 2x4s.  For heavier duty shelving with better moisture control, look for red toned 2x4 boards
cut_list: 4 - 2x4 @ 72" - legs (cut the 12' 2x4 boards in half to make these)
8 - 2x4 @ 96" - long supports (use the 8' 2x4 boards for these, check the ends for square and cut to length)
4 - 1/2" plywood @ 24" wide x 96" long (rip the plywood sheets in half with a circular saw)

steps.body:
Find a flat, level surface to build the shelving.  Mark the location of the joints, remembering the 2x4s are 3-1/2" wide.
Lay out the legs and the long supports.
Attach with 3" screws, so the frame pieces are all flush on the bottom.
Repeat to build two identical.
Join the two frames together with the 21" pieces, using two screws per joint.  Reinforce the corner joints with additional screws into the long supports.
Place the 1/2" plywood on top of the shelving.  Screw down, using the plywood to square up the shelving, with 1-3/4" screws.
For heavier loads, add 1x4s as shown to support the center of the shelving.

title: Easiest Shiplap Fireplace Tutorial with Free Plans and Video
shopping_list: 6 - 2x4 @ 10 feet long (cut 57" boards from these boards)
shiplap boards total of 56 square feet of coverage, 12 lengths ideal for maximum board use
3/4" quarter round or shoe moulding if gap at ceiling needs to be covered
54" long 4x6 beam for mantle, also used 4 - 6" long lag screws for attach mantle from back
2-1/2" long self tapping star bit screws (pack of 100)
electric fireplace insert, as shown 36" wide x 17 tall (opening was 33" x 16") - I used this one
cut_list: 2 - 2x4 @ 28-1/2" (or desired height of fireplace top of opening MINUS 1-1/2")
2 - 2x4 @ 62" (measure and cut to fit if other modifications are made)
2 - 2x4 @ 24" - blocking for supporting a heavy mantle is using
6 - 2x4 @ 12" - or desired depth of the fireplace bumpout
Beam as shown is 54" long, 4x6 beam

steps.body:
Measure overall ceiling height.  The framing should be 1" less in overall height to allow for installation.
Cut the sides to match the overall desired height.
BACK WALL FRAMING
Build the back framing wall with 2-1/2" screws.
FRONT WALL FRAMING
Build the front wall framing.  The center green 2x4 board is placed at the desired top height of the fireplace insert.  From there, fill in remaining boards to create the fireplace insert opening framing, following fireplace insert's recommendations.
For the upper portion, place the 2x4s (light blue in diagram) so they match your TV mounting bracket.

Attach the back wall framing to studs in the wall with 2-1/2" screws through the horizontal 2x4s on the back.
Use a level to make sure the back wall framing is attached level to the wall.
Then add the side 2x4s as shown.  These 2x4s set the desired depth of the fireplace bump out.
Then attach the front wall framing to the side 2x4s.
Use the 2-1/2" screws.

If additional blocking to support a heavy mantle or a center supported TV bracket, add those pieces in now.
The support blocking bottom should rest on the horizontal 2x4, to bring the weight load down to the floor.

Shiplap Installation
Start at the bottom and set the first row using a level.
Attach with 1-1/4" brad nails, two nails per stud.
Work upwards, cutting around the fireplace opening.
TIP: You can trace the fireplace opening on the inside, with the board held in place, then remove the board and cut out with a jigsaw.
Install the mantle as you work upwards while you still have access to the back of the project.  There are methods to install mantles from the front but this is easiest way.

Trim installation:
First, install the top and bottom side trim pieces, cut to fit.
Then install the top and bottom front trim pieces, cut to fit.
Then install the vertical side trim pieces.  The front side pieces need to match the plane of the front of the shiplap so a corner works out perfectly when the front trim is added.
Finally, install the front vertical trim pieces, with the corner perfectly matched.
Use 1-1/4" brad nails.
Fill all holes with wood filler and let dry.
Sand lightly with 120 grit sandpaper.
Remove all sanding residue.
Paint or finish as desired.

Install the tv and make sure that it is functioning.  Once the fireplace insert is installed, the access is gone.
Then install the fireplace insert.
The fireplace insert is easy to remove if you need to get back inside the framing.

Modification Recommendations for Angled Ceiling
An angled ceiling will definitely produce a few more challenges, but it is still achievable.
The main hurdle will be the front wall framing.
Once you get the framing done, everything else is just cut to fit over the frame.

title: House Numbers Vertical Planter from Cedar Fence Pickets
shopping_list: 1 Cedar Fence picket, 5-1/2" wide x 72" long (can be dog eared)
Screws for attaching to exterior wall (fastener type will depend on your siding/wall type)
cut_list: 1 - cedar fence picket @ 36" long - back - can be adjusted to fit your house numbers' height
3 - cedar fence pickets @ 4-3/4" long - bottom and sides
1 - cedar fence picket @ 7" - cut to fit, front piece

steps.body: Using a brad nailer and 1-1/4" galvanized nails, attach bottom to the back.  You could also use exterior wood screws, 1-1/2" to 2" long.
Nail sides to both bottom and back.
Cut front piece to fit and nail to sides and bottom.

Attach house numbers to the back piece as instructed by house number packaging.
Drill holes in bottom of planter box with drill bits.
Hang planter on exterior wall.

title: Double Cedar Planter Bench - Free Plans
description: */ /*-->*/ /*-->*/ DIY Planter Bench Plans – Cedar Fence Picket Outdoor Bench With Planter Boxes
shopping_list: 16 - cedar fence pickets 5-1/2" wide x 72" long (NOT dogeared)
About 60 - 2-3/4" self tapping general construction screws, star bit
About 200 - 1-5/8" self tapping exterior screws, star bit
cut_list: 32 - cedar fence pickets @ 24" - ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point measurement
4 - 1x3 (or cedar fence pickets ripped in half) @ 24" - ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point measurement

steps.body: Build four frames identical, using the longer screws and 2x4s.
To all the frames, attach cedar fence pickets.
Attach two frames together with remaining fence pickets to create the planter outsides.
Either use ripped cedar fence pickets or 1x3s to trim out the top of the planters.

Build the frame for between the planters independently. Attach the frame in between the planters.  The frame must be attached to the inner 2x4 frame.  If you need to add blocking to get the seat at the preferred placement, add blocking.
Attach fence pickets to the tops with the shorter screws.

title: Square Picnic Table
shopping_list: About 170 - 2-1/2" to 2-3/4" long self tapping exterior wood screws, star-bit self drilling recommended
cut_list: 8 - 2x4 @ 18-3/8" - both ends cut at 45 degrees, ends are parallel, long point to short point measurement

steps.body:
Screw the 2x6 leg piece to the 2x4 leg piece with four screws, outside edge is flush.  Best face of both boards should be faced inward.
Build four identical.

Attach the
2x4 leg side
to the
shorter, 2x4 apron (36-1/2" long)
with two screws per end.  This is important, as it may affect the overall tabletop size. Double check to make sure the overall leg width is 47-1/2" from outside of leg to outside of leg after attaching.
Slightly angling the screws will create more strength in the joint.

Repeat with the
longer (37-1/2") from the two 2x6
sides of the legs.
Double check that the tabletop dimensions are 47-1/2" x 47-1/2" at the widest part of the legs.
Build four of the seat supports by attaching the longer 2x4 to the shorter 2x4s.

TIP: Cut two blocks 13" and use as a spacer to help set the seat supports in the right height.
Attach the seat supports to each of the legs.
Make sure the shorter seat support piece (22") is attached to the 2x6 part of the leg, and the longer (23") seat support piece is attach to the 2x4 side.

Cut the 2x4s at a 45 degree angle.
Set in place and attach first at the top to the underside of the seat support.  Then attach to the legs on the inside with multiple screws.

Adjust the table frame for square.
Attach the tabletop boards to the frame with screws.
On the end boards, add extra screws into the aprons and tops of the legs, the end boards are structural.
Add the seat boards to the seat supports.
Optional seat bracing idea

title: Narrow Pantry Shelving
shopping_list: 1x3 for shelf supports (can use furring strips, about $1.50 each), equal to linear feet of shelving
1x12s, 15-3/4" wide shelving, or other shelf material for shelving
2-1/2" Cabinet Screws for attaching 1x3s to wall (#8 Preferred)
1-1/4" Cabinet Screws for attaching shelving to 1x3s and legs to shelving (#8 Preferred)

steps.body:
Mark Studs for Bottom Shelf
Use a stud finder to mark the studs in the wall, at about the height that your bottom shelf will be installed.
BEGINNER TIP: Studs are about 1-1/2" wide, try to mark the center of the stud.

Attach Bottom Shelf Supports
Cut 1x3 board about 1/4" less than the overall closet width.
Attach 1x3 board to the studs in the wall with 2-1/2" cabinet screws, one screw per stud.
Use a level to make sure the 1x3 is attached level.

Use First Row as A Pattern for Upper Rows
Since studs in walls run vertically, you can use the first 1x3 screw pattern for all subsequent 1x3 supports on the same wall.
Simply place a second 1x3 on top of the first 1x3 (as shown below) -
Then
start
screws directly above where screws are located in the first 1x3, but do NOT screw all the way through the 1x3.
Then move the second 1x3 (with screws not fully driven) to the location of the next shelf, and finish driving the screws into the studs in the wall.  Make sure you use your level to install the 1x3 level.
Repeat for additional shelves.

Repeat Supports for Ends
The same steps can be used to 1x3 supports all the way around the closet interior.
TIP: If no stud is located on tight spots (like shown), screws driven at an angle into corner studs or header studs are sufficient.

Finished Supports
The finished 1x3 supports will look like this.

Main Shelving
Now it's time to add the shelving.
Cut the shelving about 1/4" less in overall length of the inside of the closet.
Place on top of the 1x3s, working from the bottom upward.
Screw down with 1-1/4" cabinet screws.

Side Shelving
If you are wrapping the shelving around the closet, measure and cut the shelving lengths.
You will need to join the two shelving pieces together at the inside corner.
Use a 2-1/2" screw at an angle, a 2" nail at an angle, or you can use a 1-1/4" pocket hole screw and your Kreg Jig.

Front Supports
Front supports will help strengthen the shelf and keep the boards straight.
Attach a 1x3 leg to the front, as needed.
Make sure the 1x3 is plumb (level vertically)
Cut the 1x3 to the height of the top shelf
Make sure the shelf is level as you attach it to the 1x3 (not sagging front to back)
Make sure the 1x3 leg is all the way on the ground
Use a 1-1/4" cabinet screw to attach the leg to the shelf
Legs should be placed about every 36" for 3/4" thick shelving

title: Sturdy Outdoor Side Table Stool
shopping_list: 51" of cedar fence picket per stool
Apply stain to sanded boards and let dry.

steps.body: Build two leg sets identical.  Use a 1/2" piece of scrap wood to elevate the apron up so it is inset 1/2" on the leg when you attach it.  Use the 2-3/4" star bit self tapping screws.
Attach the leg sets together with the 13" pieces.
Attach the fence pickets to the top and screw down.

title: American Girl Dollhouse - Fits 18" Dolls
shopping_list: 6 - 1x4 @ 12 feet long (add another one for the base if desired)
cut_list: 3 - 3/4" plywood or MDF @ 23-3/4" x 73" (sides and center divider)
5 - 1x4 @ 49-1/2" (OR 1- 3/4" plywood @ 49-1/2" x 17-1/2" (measure and cut to fit))

steps.body: First, cut out all the pieces. If you don't have a truck or tablesaw, it might be easier to have your home improvement store rip your plywood into 22-3/4" widths, 8 feet long. Then cut the longest pieces first. The top roof angle is 45 degrees. You can easily find this by finding the center of your side and middle panels (at the top). This distance should be 11-7/8" from the sides. Then measure down 11-7/8", and draw a diagonal between the two points. Then mark out all windows and doors and cut out with a jigsaw.
Once the pieces are cut, attach the floor to the walls. I used a Kreg Jig set at 3/4" and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.
Next, add the floors. I used 3/4" pocket holes on the underside and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.

Because we opted for one large room on the top floor, I opted to use plywood for my roof (no center support without the divided upper room). Keep top edge flush as you attach room with glue and finish nails or staples.
For the roof front, we just used 1x boards.  If first attached together with pocket holes, and then attached the entire roof panel on top.
For the back, I used 1/4" plywood and nailed it to all of the walls and floors with glue.
We opted for caster wheels instead, but you could also finish out the base with 1x boards. Shown in the diagram is 1x4s.
I measured each window and built the window trim to fit snugly, gluing in place when I was done.
I haven't added shutters yet, but this is what 1x2 shutters would look like.

title: Modern Park Bench
shopping_list: 60 - 2-1/2" to 3" long exterior self tapping screws (look for the star bit ones)
60 - 1-1/2" to 2" long exterior self tapping screws (look for the star bit ones)

steps.body:
Wood glue is recommended at all joints.
Build the basic seat frame of the swing first, using the longer screws, two screws per joint.
Next, add the legs to the seat frame using the longer screws, three screws per joint.
Then add the arms on top of the shorter legs, and then tie into longer back legs, using the longer screws, two screws per joint.
Add the shorter stretchers between the bottom legs - this will help keep the legs from splaying out. Use the longer screws, two screws per joint.
Attach bottom stretcher between legs, centered, and attach with two screws per end, using the longer screws.
Attach backrest supports with the longer screws, two screws per joint.
Lay out the seat boards on top of the bench and attach with the shorter screws, two screws per joint.

CUT YOUR STRINGERS
From the 1×8 stringers listed in the cut list, use a speed square and carefully mark out the cuts needed with a pencil on the board.
After all cuts are made, clamp the board down securely and cut off with a circular saw.
Repeat for the second stringer.
FRONT APRON Attach your front apron with 2″ screws and glue as shown above.
SEAT SLATS Lay your seat slats as shown above, starting with the first flat seat slat. Leave a 1/2″ gap between the slats. Screw down with 2″ screws and glue.
FRONT LEGS Use 1 1/4″ screws and glue to attach the front legs as shown above.
BACK Build the back as shown above.

ATTACHING THE BACK Attach the back as shown above. This isn’t an exact science, I purposely choose to use a 1×3 for the arm supports and a 2×2 for the back support so that it would be super easy for you to get the back to line up just right with the seat and arm supports. You may want to enlist your neighbors assistance.
ARMS & SUPPORTS Attach your arms with 2″ screws and glue. Then attach the arm supports.

title: Folding Sewing Table
shopping_list: 1 – Sheet of 3/4" thick sanded plywood or hardwood plywood
4 – 2″ Caster Wheels (Overall clearance of 2 1/2″) and screws for attaching caster wheels to the base (3/4" length most likely with a larger head)
5 – Sets of simple hinges (2-1/2" to 3" length) - ten total hinges
2" self tapping wood screws (spax or similar, can use trim screws)

steps.body:
Lay the plywood out on top of scrap 1x boards or styrofoam.
Mark the plywood every 32" width wise with pencil lines. Set circular saw to depth slightly greater than thickness of plywood.
Cut on the pencil lines to create three equal sized pieces, each 32" x 48".
From the 32" wide pieces, cut down as shown in the diagram and cut list.
Assemble the main box as shown in the diagram, using the 2" screws.
Attach wheels to base of the cabinet with the caster wheel screws.
Position the cabinet face down on the floor and place the leaves next to the cabinet.  Attach on underside with the simple hinges.

Measure the overall distance from the underside of the cabinet to the wheel bottom and cut the 1x3s to this length.
Attach legs to hinges and to the underside of the leaves.  You can also use a fold out hinge for added security.

Flip the table over and test the leg lengths.  Cut down if necessary.
You want the legs to open slightly past 90 degrees in opposite angles, so the legs are slightly splayed out, to keep the project from collapsing.
You can also use table leg hinges.

title: Fence Picket Bench Planter
shopping_list: 4 - Fence Pickets, dog-eared and 72" long x 5-1/2" wide
48"  of 1x2 or rip fence picket in half with what is left after all cuts have been made
cut_list: I recommend cutting all of the back pieces with dog ear sections first and then make your straight cuts once those are done.
4 - 22.5" bottom and front and back of box
bottom of box support - 2 - 1x2 - about 11.5" ( I ripped the scraps of a fence picket)
back of vertical slats support 1-1x2 @ 20" ( I used a scrap 1x2) can use ripped fence picket here too

steps.body:
Start by building the arms by attaching the front leg and back legs to the side support.
You will build two legs, but they must be in MIRROR to each other.
Attach the top of the arm rest to the back leg and front leg.

Attach the side supports to the front and back pieces with the nails.
If you have shorter nails (3/4"), attach the legs to the front and back pieces as well.
Place the 1x2 pieces at the bottom of the planter and attach from all sides with the 1-1/4" nails.
Place the floor pieces inside the planter. You can nail from outsides if desired, with the 1-1/4" nails.
Place back pieces between back legs, with even gaps in between.  Attach to the back with the 3/4" nails.
Attach the 1x2 back support to the back with the 1-1/4" nails, nail from the front.

title: Homestead Coffee Table - Free DIY Furniture Plans
shopping_list: *use light yellow wood tone for indoor projects, red toned wood boards for exterior
cut_list: Modification I made in my build - I used two 1x12 @ 42" for the top
(4) 2x4 @ 13-1/4″, 10° angle cuts on both ends (parallel) - long point to short point measurement
(2) 2x4 @ 14-5/8″, 10° angle cuts on both ends (NOT parallel), long point to long point measurement
(2) 2x4 @ 17-7/8″, 10° angle cuts on both ends (NOT parallel), long point to long point measurement
(2) 2x6 @ 20-3/4″, 10° angle cuts on both ends (NOT parallel), long point to long point measurement

steps.body:
Build two leg sets identical.  Your coffee table will look much better if you hide joinery using a pocket hole jig, but you can also use 2-1/2" self tapping screws to build.
TIP: As done in the video, it is much easier to build this by attaching first with 2" brad nails and glue.  Then do the pocket holes.
Attach base plate with the self tapping screws.

position the legs on the underside of the tabletop boards. Attach legs to the tabletop boards.
TIP: For a nicer top, build top first with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws.
Place the bottom shelf boards inside the coffee table.  Attach from underneath with the 2-1/2" screws.

title: Simple Panel Bed (All Mattress Sizes) - No Pocket Holes
shopping_list: SHOPPING LIST FOR QUEEN - for other sizes, see the downloaded PDFs above
2 - 1x8 @ 10 feet long (can cut in half for transport)
11 - 2x4 @ 8 feet long (includes 6 2x4s used for the bed slats)
50 - 1-1/4" brad nails OR 2" self tapping wood screws
40 - 3" self tapping wood screws (can substitute for 2-1/2" or 2-3/4" lengths)
cut_list: CUT LIST FOR QUEEN - for other sizes, see the downloaded PDFs above
1 - 2x4 @ ~57" - cut to fit - headboard bed frame
18 - 2x4 @ 31-1/2" - bed frame slats (can also use 1x4s or 1x3s or other materials if less expensive/have on hand)

steps.body:
Layout the two headboard legs.
Mark a line all the way down each 2x4 leg, 2-1/4" in from the outsides.
Dryfit the panel boards for the headboard on the legs.
Then attach the top 1x8 to the legs, with glue and screws or nails.
Repeat, working downward, for all the 1x8s - just make sure you apply a bead of glue between the 1x8 boards as you go.

Apply glue to all top edges of the headboard.
Place 1x3 on top, even overhang on ends, flush to the back 1x8 on back.
Nail or screw down - nails are easier to hide, so use if you have a brad nailer.
Repeat steps for footboard, with only one panel board used.

Apply glue to 2x4 cleats and place 1x8 siderail on top.  Screw or nails siderail to cleat form outside.
Nails will be easier to hide, so use if you can.

Attach the siderails to the headboard and footboard with 3" screws.
A spacer block cut from scrap 2x4s will make assembly much easier.
Screw the headboard bed frame piece to the headboard.
Attach bed frame legs to center bed frame support with 3" screws.
Screw bed frame center to the headboard and footboard with 3" screws, centered on the bed.

Place slats on bed frame, space about 4" apart.
Screw down using the 3" screws.

title: Grandy Barn Door Console
shopping_list: 1 sheet of 1/4" thick plywood or hardboard (for back)
2 - project panel pieces 17-1/4" x 36" (may be advertised as 18" wide project panel - measure in store)
cut_list: 2 - 3/4" thick project panels or plywood 36" x 17-1/4"
1 - 1x2 @ 90" - both ends cut at 45 degrees, NOT parallel, longest point measurement
2 - 1x2 @ 18-3/4" - one end cut at 45 degrees, longest point measurement
Doors are cut to fit, overall 20" x 30"

steps.body:
Build two frames out of 2x3s.  The frames should be screwed together as this is the main support for the entire piece.  Use 2-1/2" or longer screws, or for those of you with a Kreg Jig, use 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws.  Don't forget to glue the joints.
Don't be concrened about being perfect here, the entire frame is concealed.

For the sides, add the side panels.  This plan is designed to work with project panels 17-1/4" wide.  You can use any material here, plywood or reclaimed wood too.  I recommend screwing these to the frames, but you could also use nails and glue - nails should be at least 1-1/4" long.  I love how the back overhangs by 1/4" to conceal the back (will put that on in later steps).

Now the face frame.  With the projec ton it's back, lay out all the face frame boards and glue and nail down.  Nails should be at least 1-1/4" long. Pocket hole users could also build the face frame seperately and attach.

Lay the bottom shelf boards down on the bottom frame.  Nail and glue down, with nails longer than 1-1/4".  There can be a gap between the boards, but the shelf should not go past the back of the bottom frame (sides overhang by 1/4" to allow for the back).
Build your bottom shelf by attaching bottom shelf boards to the shelf cleats.  Use 1-1/4" long nails and glue to attach.  Can have a gap in the middle.
Place shelf inside console and attach from outsides to secure in place.

To get the planked look on the back of the console, Nikki ripped 1/4" plywood into strips and then attached the individual strips to the back.  You can also use full sheet of 1/4" plywood. You can use shorter nails here but if you don't want to buy a shorter nail (3/4" would be ideal) the 1-1/4" nails would be fine.
The bottom is trimmed out in simple 1x2s, mitered around corners.  Use 1-1/4" nails and glue to secure.
Attach top with the longer screws you used to build the top frames (2-1/2" or longer) so top is flush to back and side overhangs are even.  TIP: You may wish to stain the top boards first.

Nikki built the doors shown with 1/2" plywood with smaller strips nailed on top, but you can use a variety of different methods to build the doors (love the reclaimed wood!) Nikki makes her own sliding door hardware too.
Jaime from That's My Letter provides a good tutorial on her sliding door hardware if you want to check that out.

title: Fancy X Farmhouse Table
cut_list: 8 – 2x4 @ 11 3/8” (ends cut at 45 degrees off square, longest point measurement, NOT parallel)
2 – 2x4 @ 30 3/8” (both ends cut parallel at 45 degrees off square)
** For 45 degree cut tops and bottoms (easier) on the legs top and bottom (see steps 3-5 and step 10), replace these cuts with:
4 - 2x4 @ 31" (both ends cut at 45 degrees off square NOT parallel long point measurement)
4 - 2x4 @ 34" (both ends cut at 45 degrees off square NOT parallel long point measurement)

steps.body: Build four of these. I’d simply countersink screws from the backs into the ends of the cross braces using 2-1/2" self tapping wood screws
Then just attach two of the leg pieces together with glue and 2” finish nails from each side
And add the top/bottom. You can use 2” screws or nails here and glue.

And then add the curved pieces. The ends may be a challenge – what I do is first cut a 2x4 32 5/8” long with both ends cut at 30 degrees off square, ends not parallel. Then make a second cut at 45 (or more if your saw cuts higher degree bevels) degrees off square and cut that same board 28 3/8”  short point to short point.
Repeat for the next layer of 2xs to build up your leg ends
Followed by the little feet ...
I'd recommend 1 1/2" PHs and 2 1/2" PH screws here ... hide on top and bottom edges

The easiest way to attach cross braces is with pocket holes.  But you will have to fill later with wood filler.
An alternative is to glue and screw through the cross brace into the top and bottom 2x4s with longer screws.

And finally the top! I recommend building the tabletop first with pocket holes and then attaching.
If you are using the table outdoors, leave a little space between the boards for water drainage.
And for the alternate ends like Whitney did - just use 45 degrees off square cuts.

For more photos and construction details, please stop over and visit
Whitney at Shanty2Chic!

title: Wood Tilt Out Trash or Recycling Cabinet
shopping_list: Narrow Hinges (the cheapo kind with flat headed screws)
Wire or toybox lid support hinge to keep the door from opening all the way to the floor

steps.body:
Use either a
Kreg Jig
or 2" screws or brad nails to build your box as shown above. The bottom shelf is 2 1/2" from the bottom, at the top. So leave 1 3/4" gap underneath the bottom shelf. Check for square.

Once your box is done, attach back supports to keep the project square.
NOTE: If you would like to access your trash from the back to remove, hold off on placing the center back support until after you install the tilt out door.
Attach front trim with brad nails to the front of the cabinet.
Build the door frame with 1x3s using pocket holes.

Drill pocket holes along bottom edge of 1x12 bottom piece.
Attach to the frame, inset 1" from edges, but held low 1/8" on bottom edge, with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.

Cut the sides at a 45 degree angle.
Drill two pocket holes on sides.
Attach to door panel and to the bottom.
Cut the 1/4" plywood panel to fit inside the door panel.  Attach with 3/4" brad nails and glue.

Hinge the door to the cabinet. For our tilt out trash cabinet/recycle center, we didn't even have to worry about a magnetic catch - the weight of the tilt out base keeps everything closed nicely.
However, you may need to install either hardware or a chain to keep the tilt out door from opening all the way and bruising your toes.

title: DIY Bunk Bed Steps with Built-In Drawers – Easy Build with Free Plans
shopping_list: 1-1/4" self tapping star bit wood screws
*If the width of the stairs was reduced by 1/4", you could use a 1x12 x 10' instead of 12'.
** Cut longest boards to for best wood efficiency
cut_list: 4 - 1x10 @ 24" - rip 1-1/2" off for the cross framing pieces and reserve remaining piece for drawer faces
8 - 1x6 @ 18-1/2" - drawer front and backs, all drawers
3 - 1/4" plywood @ 20" x 24" - bottom three drawers, bottoms

steps.body:
Layout the 1x10 pieces with the longest on the bottom, stacking upward.  Apply glue in between boards.
Attach 1x3s on top, outside edges are flush, with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails.
Build two in mirror.

Measure down 7" from top of each row and mark.
Attach drawer slides so the holes in the drawer slides are centered on the 7" lines, and the screws are started in the lines.
the top drawer slide is the 20" slide.
Add blocking if needed.
Here is a video of installing these drawer slides:

Cut the 1x10 pieces at 24".
Rip off pieces at 1-1/2".  Reserve the scrap as a drawer face in later steps.
Attach 1x2s as shown to the two sides of the bunk bed stairs, use glue and brad nails.

Add the 1x12 treads with glue and brad nails, making sure the outside edges are flush.
Make sure you apply glue and attach from the 1x2 at the back as well.
I also added some screws for increases support.
Attach the back 1/4" plywood with 3/4" brad nails and wood glue, keeping outside edges flush.

Build the drawer boxes with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue.
Attach drawer bottoms with 3/4" brad nails and wood glue.
Install the drawer slides on the drawers.  Make sure the drawers slide smoothly.
Then add the drawer faces first with brad nails, followed by 1-1/4" screws from the insides.
If needed, sand drawer face edges for a perfect slide.
After drawer are installed, additional blocking can be added to reinforce if needed. Tip on side and note where the drawers are located, and add the blocking (if needed).

title: Large Wooden Milk Crate Tutorial using Cedar Fence Pickets
shopping_list: 2 - cedar fence pickets, 5-1/2" wide x 6 feet long
cut_list: 6 - 1x2 @ 10-1/2" - legs and bottom fill in pieces
4 - cedar fence pickets @ 13" - front and back

steps.body:
Cut the 1x2s with a circular saw or compound miter saw.
Nail the vertical 1x4s to the bottom 1x4 with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue.
Build two identical.
Crate depth adjustment: The bottom 1x2 sets the depth for the crate.  For custom size, cut 1" less than the overall desired depth of your crate.
Crate height adjustment: The vertical 1x2s set the depth of the crate.  Cut 3/4" less than the overall desired height.  As shown, height is designed to work with 2 cedar fence pickets.
Cut the cedar fence pickets for the sides and nail to the 1x2 inner frame.

Width Adjustment: The front and back cedar fence pickets set the width of the crate.  Cut to the desired width of the crate.
Attach the cedar fence pickets to the front and back with the 1" brad nails.
Inside - fill in the insides between the 1x2s with 1x2 pieces.
Inside - fill in the remaining inside with cedar fence pickets.

title: Laundry Basket Dresser
shopping_list: 3/4″ Plywood cut into 15 1/2″ wide x 8 feet long strips (referred to as 1x16s)
1 1/2″ x 1 1/2″ metal angle (ask for it at Blue or Orange)
2″ wood screws or 1 1/4″ pocket hole screws 3/4″ wood screws (to screw the metal angle to the insides of the boxes)
cut_list: 6 – 1 1/2″ x 1 1/2″ Metal Angle 15 1/2″ long (ask hardware store to cut or cut with a hack saw)

steps.body:
Build the Box
I used pocket holes, but you can also use 2″ wood screws countersunk and screwed together to build the box.
NOTE: Top Measurement should be 26″

Back
Adjust for square then screw the back on with 1 1/4″ screws and glue.  I finished mine at this stage by filling all holes with wood filler, all plywood edges, and any imperfections in the plywood.  I then sanded with coarse sandpaper because the plywood was builder grade cheapo stuff, the finished with medium sandpaper.  Finally, I primed and painted with ooops paint in semigloss from Valspar.

Metal Angle
If you are intimidated by metal angle, you can always use 2x2s glued and screwed here.  Cut the metal angle with a hack saw and predrill holes (three per slide).  Mark locations with a square and screw metal angle to the sides.  TIP: Screw at a very slight downward angle to keep the baskets to the back of the box instead of sliding forward.
If you choose to stack, make sure you secure the top laundry basket dress to a stud in the wall.

title: Cedar Vertical Tiered Ladder Garden Planter
shopping_list: 5 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets (dogeared is fine) 72" length
20 - 2-1/2" long exterior decking screws (for attaching boxes to legs)
1-1/4" galvanized nails or staples (for making boxes)
cut_list: 10 - 2x6 @ 5-1/4" both ends cut at 15 degrees off square, ends are NOT parallel
5 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 20-1/2" (cut to fit)
2 - 2x6 @ 68-3/8" both ends cut at 15 degrees off square, ends ARE parallel

steps.body: Attach two of the longer cedar fence picket pieces to two ends. I used 1-1/4" galvanized staples, but you could also use screws or nails.
I placed the bottoms in from the top, and then added a few staples to keep them in place.

The sides are simply cut with ends parallel at 15 degrees, then the back is cut off perpendicular to the top 15 degree cut. It's easier than you'd think. I used a jigsaw but the right tool would have been a circular saw. Start your cut on the top as you will have more to bit into.

Mark the placement of the top planter on both legs first to make sure you place the planter in exactly the same spot on both legs. Then I attached from inside with 2-1/2" exterior decking screws. The 2x6s I used were very hard to drill into, so I ended up predrilling as well.

title: Modern Fence Picket Planter or Raised Bed – Multiple Sizes – Free DIY Plans
shopping_list: 2-3/4" deck screws for building inner frame
cedar fence pickets (72" x 5-1/2", not dogeared) for the exterior covering and "floor"
1-5/8" deck screws for attaching cedar to inner frames
Top trim is cedar fence picket ripped in half - but you could use a 1x3 board as an alternative
cut_list: Planters can be made in different sizes (up to 72" if using fence pickets).  The following sizes and cut lists are optimized for efficient use of materials
72" x 36" - Raised Garden Bed (3 Boards Tall) 18" height (four inner supports)
This size is great for larger garden beds, with two or three rows of plants, accessible from all sides
6 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 72" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel
6 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 36" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel
5 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 70" long - planter "floor"
2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 72" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim
2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 36" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim
72" x 24" - Raised Garden Bed (3 Boards Tall) 18" height (four inner supports)
This size is slightly narrower, could still hold two rows of plants (depending on the plants)
6 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 72" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel
6 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 24" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel
4 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 70" long - planter "floor"
2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 72" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim
2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 24" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim
72" x 18" - Narrow Planter (3 Boards Tall) 18" height (four inner supports)
Long and narrow, great for under windows, or single rows of plants
6 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 72" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel
6 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 18" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel
3 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 70" long - planter "floor"
2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 72" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim
2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 18" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim
72" x 18" - Narrow 4 Board Tall Planter (23" height) (four inner supports)
Long and narrow, slightly taller, great for under windows
8 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 72" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel
8 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 18" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel
3 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 70" long - planter "floor"
2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 72" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim

8 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 24" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel

6 - 5-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 24" long - both ends beveled at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel

2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 24" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim
2 - 2-1/2" wide cedar fence pickets @ 24" long - both ends mitered at 45 degrees, long point to long point, ends NOT parallel - top trim
